photo_description: The exterior of a residential or small public building showing a concrete accessibility ramp with white metal handrails alongside a traditional staircase, leading to the building entrance from street level.

findings.observation: A concrete accessibility ramp is present alongside the staircase, providing a gradual slope from street level up to the building entrance. The ramp appears to be a purpose-built retrofit.
findings.estimated_measure: approximately 4–6 m in length, with a rise of approximately 40–60 cm

findings.observation: White metal handrails are installed on both sides of the ramp, running the full length of the ramp. The handrails appear to be at a standard height.
findings.estimated_measure: handrail height approximately 85–100 cm from ramp surface

findings.observation: A traditional staircase with approximately 3–4 steps is present to the left of the ramp, leading to the same entrance level. Steps appear to have no tactile warning strips visible.
The ramp gradient appears moderate; visually it looks steeper than the recommended 1:12 (≈8%) gradient for wheelchair accessibility, which could pose a challenge for independent wheelchair users.
The ramp surface is smooth concrete, which may present a slip risk when wet as no anti-slip texture or strips are visibly applied to the ramp surface.
The approach from the street is asphalt, which appears relatively smooth and level with no significant surface breaks or obstacles visible.
The ramp width appears sufficient for a single wheelchair, though the exact clearance between the handrails limits the usable width.
No visible accessibility signage (ISA symbol or directional wayfinding) is observed directing users to the ramp.
